checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the P2P gossip validation layer validate commitment_indices bounds before passing to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl?

reachability.notes: Out-of-bounds commitment_indices would cause array index out-of-bounds access, potentially crashing the node.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Trace the data flow from P2P message reception to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl. 2. Identify all validation layers mentioned: 'P2P gossip validation' and 'Length checks'. 3. Verify that P2P gossip validation explicitly checks: len(commitment_indices) <= MAX_ALLOWED and max(commitment_indices) < num_unique_commitments_in_batch. 4. Check if length checks validate relationship between commitment_indices length and commitments length. 5. Identify the deduplication step: does it happen before or after bounds validation? 6. If validation occurs after deduplication: verify bounds check uses deduplicated array length, not original length. 7. If validation occurs before deduplication: verify bounds check still holds after deduplication reduces array size. 8. Craft test case: submit batch where commitment_indices are valid pre-deduplication but invalid post-deduplication.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-8, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Deduplication may reduce commitments array size, making previously-valid indices out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer cause validator node crash by sending crafted commitment_indices exceeding array bounds?

reachability.notes: Out-of-bounds commitment_indices would cause array index out-of-bounds access, potentially crashing the node.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Set up test validator node with crash monitoring/logging enabled. 2. Connect as P2P peer to the validator node. 3. Construct batch KZG proof verification request with: a) Valid commitments array of length N, b) commitment_indices containing value >= N. 4. Send the crafted request via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor validator node for: a) Crash/panic (vulnerability confirmed), b) Error response and continued operation (properly handled), c) Silent failure (investigate further). 6. Vary the out-of-bounds index: N, N+1, MAX_UINT, negative values (if signed). 7. Test with empty commitments array (N=0) and any positive index. 8. Document exact crash location (function, line) if crash occurs. 9. Verify if multiple validators can be crashed simultaneously (network-wide DoS potential).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-8, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Bug bounty eligible: Requires PoC demonstrating node crash via crafted commitment_indices.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: What is the memory safety impact of out-of-bounds commitment_indices access in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl?

reachability.notes: Out-of-bounds commitment_indices would cause array index out-of-bounds access, potentially crashing the node.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Determine the implementation language of verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch_impl (Rust, Go, C, etc.). 2. Assess memory safety model: a) Rust: panic on out-of-bounds (DoS, not memory corruption), b) Go: panic on out-of-bounds (DoS, not memory corruption), c) C/C++: undefined behavior (potential memory corruption, RCE). 3. If unsafe language (C/C++): check for explicit bounds validation or use of safe array abstractions. 4. Analyze what data could be read if bounds check is missing: a) Adjacent heap data (info disclosure), b) Function pointers (potential RCE if used), c) Sensitive cryptographic material. 5. Check if the indexed commitment is used in subsequent cryptographic operations: could corrupted data cause signature verification bypass? 6. Verify compiler/runtime protections: stack canaries, ASLR, bounds checking sanitizers in production builds.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-8, Source Invariant: SG-015:INV-004. Severity could escalate to Critical if memory corruption leads to RCE in unsafe language implementations.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Are all four input arrays (commitment_bytes, cell_indices, cells, proofs_bytes) validated for equal length in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: Mismatched array lengths from P2P input could cause out-of-bounds access or incorrect verification. This is the first validation checkpoint.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) function in the codebase. 2. Identify the length validation logic at the function entry point. 3. Verify that the function explicitly checks: len(commitment_bytes) == len(cell_indices) == len(cells) == len(proofs_bytes) BEFORE any cryptographic operations. 4. Confirm the validation occurs BEFORE any array indexing or iteration. 5. Check that mismatched lengths result in immediate rejection with appropriate error code. 6. Via P2P, craft a batch verification request with intentionally mismatched array lengths: a) commitment_bytes.len=5, cell_indices.len=4, cells.len=5, proofs_bytes.len=5, b) All arrays empty except one with single element, c) One array significantly larger than others. 7. Submit via P2P gossip and verify each malformed request is rejected before cryptographic processing. 8. Monitor for crashes, hangs, or unexpected behavior when processing mismatched inputs.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-002. Attacker-controlled input lengths via P2P. First validation checkpoint - must reject before any array access.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does P2P gossip validation enforce equal-length constraint on batch KZG proof verification inputs before reaching verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: Mismatched array lengths from P2P input could cause out-of-bounds access or incorrect verification. This is the first validation checkpoint.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Trace the data flow from P2P message reception to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 2. Identify the 'P2P gossip validation' layer mentioned in reachability. 3. Check if length validation occurs at the P2P layer (defense in depth) OR only at the cryptographic function level. 4. If P2P layer validates: verify it enforces len(commitment_bytes) == len(cell_indices) == len(cells) == len(proofs_bytes). 5. If P2P layer does NOT validate: verify that verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch itself performs the validation (single point of validation - higher risk). 6. Test bypass: can a malicious peer send raw bytes that deserialize into mismatched arrays, bypassing P2P-level schema validation? 7. Identify all code paths that lead to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch and verify each one performs length validation.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Defense in depth: ideally both P2P layer and function itself should validate.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer crash a validator node by sending batch KZG verification request with mismatched array lengths?

reachability.notes: Mismatched array lengths from P2P input could cause out-of-bounds access or incorrect verification. This is the first validation checkpoint.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Set up test validator node with crash monitoring and memory debugging enabled. 2. Connect as P2P peer to the validator. 3. Construct batch KZG proof verification requests with various length mismatches: a) commitment_bytes[10], cell_indices[5], cells[10], proofs_bytes[10] - shorter cell_indices, b) commitment_bytes[0], cell_indices[1], cells[1], proofs_bytes[1] - empty commitments with non-empty others, c) commitment_bytes[1000], cell_indices[1], cells[1], proofs_bytes[1] - one very large array. 4. Send each crafted request via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor validator node for: a) Crash/panic (vulnerability confirmed - DoS), b) Out-of-bounds memory access (potential info leak or RCE), c) Proper error response with continued operation (correctly handled). 6. If no length validation exists: determine what index is used for iteration (first array? largest array? smallest array?). 7. Document exact failure mode and crash location if crash occurs.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Bug bounty eligible per property: Requires PoC showing crash or incorrect verification result from mismatched input lengths.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch validate array lengths BEFORE performing any cryptographic operations or array access?

reachability.notes: Mismatched array lengths from P2P input could cause out-of-bounds access or incorrect verification. This is the first validation checkpoint.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Open verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) source code. 2. Identify the first line of code that accesses any of the four input arrays (commitment_bytes, cell_indices, cells, proofs_bytes). 3. Verify that length validation check appears BEFORE this first access. 4. Check for implicit array access: a) Iterating with 'for (i, item) in array.enumerate()' - uses first array's length implicitly, b) Zipping arrays - what happens if lengths differ? Silent truncation? Panic? 5. If using zip: verify it's 'zip_strict' or equivalent that panics on length mismatch. 6. If using iteration with index: verify index doesn't exceed shortest array length when accessing other arrays. 7. Check for early returns or computations that might access arrays before length validation. 8. Identify cryptographic operations (KZG proof verification) and verify they only occur after length validation passes.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-9, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-002. Pre-condition: validation MUST occur before crypto operations. Violation = undefined behavior on mismatched lengths.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Is each cell_index validated to be strictly less than CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB before use in coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) function in the codebase. 2. Identify where cell_indices array is accessed and iterated. 3. Verify that BEFORE any cell_index is passed to coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell, there exists an explicit bounds check: cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB. 4. Check the validation occurs BEFORE the cell_index is used to compute array offsets (e.g., roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index]). 5. Verify the bounds check applies to ALL cell_indices in the batch, not just the first one. 6. Confirm rejection behavior: out-of-range cell_index must cause immediate function return with error, not proceed to array access. 7. Via P2P, craft data column sidecar with cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB (boundary value). 8. Via P2P, craft data column sidecar with cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB + 1 (just above boundary). 9. Via P2P, craft data column sidecar with cell_index = 0xFFFFFFFF or maximum uint value. 10. Submit each via P2P gossip and verify rejection occurs BEFORE any array access in coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-004, Source Threat: stride-017. Out-of-bounds access to roots_brp array via crafted cell_index. Bug bounty eligible: Requires PoC demonstrating node crash from out-of-range cell_index in P2P data column sidecar.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does P2P gossip validation layer reject data column sidecars with cell_index >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB before reaching verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Trace the data flow from P2P message reception (data column sidecar) to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 2. Identify the 'P2P gossip validation' layer mentioned in reachability. 3. Check if cell_index bounds validation occurs at the P2P layer (defense in depth) OR only at the cryptographic function level. 4. If P2P layer validates: verify it enforces cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB for all cell_indices in the message. 5. If P2P layer does NOT validate: verify that verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch itself performs the validation (single point of validation - higher risk). 6. Test bypass: can a malicious peer send raw bytes that deserialize into cell_index >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB, bypassing P2P-level schema validation? 7. Check if there's any path where cell_index validation could be skipped (e.g., trusted peer, local import, replay). 8. Identify all code paths that lead to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch and verify each one includes cell_index bounds checking.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Defense in depth: ideally both P2P layer and function itself should validate cell_index bounds.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer crash a validator node by sending data column sidecar with out-of-bounds cell_index?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Set up test validator node with crash monitoring, memory debugging (ASAN/MSAN), and core dump collection enabled. 2. Connect as P2P peer to the validator. 3. Identify the exact constant value of CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB in the codebase. 4. Construct data column sidecars with out-of-bounds cell_indices: a) cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB (exact boundary), b) cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB + 1 (one past boundary), c) cell_index = 2 * CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB (double the valid range), d) cell_index = 0xFFFFFFFF or max uint (maximum possible value). 5. Send each crafted sidecar via P2P gossip protocol. 6. Monitor validator node for: a) Crash/segfault (vulnerability confirmed - DoS), b) AddressSanitizer out-of-bounds error (confirms memory safety issue), c) Panic/assertion failure in coset_for_cell or coset_shift_for_cell, d) Proper error response with continued operation (correctly handled). 7. If crash occurs, analyze the stack trace to confirm it originates from roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index] access. 8. Document exact failure mode, crash location, and memory address if crash occurs. 9. Verify whether the crash is reproducible (consistent DoS vector).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-validator-node. Severity: HIGH. Out-of-bounds array access in coset_shift_for_cell or coset_for_cell could crash the node (segfault) or cause memory corruption, leading to single-node DoS. Bug bounty eligible: Requires PoC demonstrating node crash from out-of-range cell_index in P2P data column sidecar.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch validate cell_index bounds BEFORE passing to coset_for_cell and coset_shift_for_cell?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Open verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) source code. 2. Locate all calls to coset_for_cell and coset_shift_for_cell functions. 3. For each call site, trace back to verify that cell_index is validated (cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) BEFORE the call. 4. Check for implicit assumptions: a) Does the function assume caller has validated cell_index? (dangerous assumption at trust boundary), b) Are there early returns or short-circuit evaluations that could skip validation? 5. Examine coset_for_cell and coset_shift_for_cell functions: do they perform their own bounds checking on cell_index? 6. Locate the array access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index]. 7. Calculate the maximum valid index: if cell_index = CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB - 1, what is FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index? Is this within roots_brp array bounds? 8. Check for integer overflow: could FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index overflow if cell_index is very large? 9. Verify that the pre-condition (cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) is explicitly enforced, not implicitly assumed.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-004. Pre-condition type property: validation MUST occur before use. The property text explicitly states 'MUST be rejected before being used'.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can integer overflow in FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index bypass bounds checking or cause unexpected array access?

reachability.notes: Attacker sends cell_indices >= CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB via P2P. coset_shift_for_cell and coset_for_cell access roots_brp[FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index], which would be out-of-bounds.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the types of cell_index and FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL constants. 2. Calculate if FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index can overflow for any valid or invalid cell_index value. 3. Determine what happens on overflow: a) Panic (safe but DoS), b) Wrap-around to smaller value (dangerous - could access valid memory with wrong data), c) Saturation (unlikely but check). 4. If overflow is possible and wraps: a) Calculate what cell_index values would wrap to indices 0..len(roots_brp)-1, b) Such values would pass array bounds checks but access wrong data. 5. Craft test cases with cell_index values that cause integer overflow in the multiplication. 6. Send via P2P and observe behavior: does it crash, reject, or silently use wrong data? 7. If the multiplication uses checked arithmetic (checked_mul), verify overflow returns error rather than proceeding. 8. Verify bounds check (cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB) occurs BEFORE the multiplication, preventing overflow entirely.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-10, Trust Boundary: tb-002. If bounds check occurs after multiplication, attacker could cause overflow. Proper order: 1) check cell_index < CELLS_PER_EXT_BLOB, 2) only then compute FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * cell_index.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Are commitment_bytes, cell, and proof_bytes lengths validated to exact constants before processing in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Trace the data flow from P2P message reception (data column sidecar) to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021). 2. Identify the 'P2P gossip validation' layer mentioned in reachability. 3. Locate where commitment_bytes, cell, and proof_bytes are first received from P2P. 4. Verify that length validation occurs BEFORE any deserialization or cryptographic operations: a) commitment_bytes.length == BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT, b) cell.length == BYTES_PER_CELL, c) proof_bytes.length == BYTES_PER_PROOF. 5. Check if validation occurs at P2P layer (defense in depth) OR only at cryptographic function level. 6. If only at function level: verify verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch performs exact length checks with explicit rejection on mismatch. 7. Test bypass: can raw P2P bytes deserialize into arrays of incorrect length, bypassing schema validation? 8. Verify all three length checks are enforced (not just some of them).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-001. Pre-condition requires exact length matching for cryptographic inputs. Incorrect lengths could cause buffer overflows or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer send commitment_bytes with incorrect length to cause crash or memory corruption?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the exact value of BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT constant in the codebase. 2. Construct data column sidecars with malformed commitment_bytes: a) commitment_bytes with length = BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT - 1 (one byte short), b) commitment_bytes with length = BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT + 1 (one byte extra), c) commitment_bytes with length = 0 (empty), d) commitment_bytes with length = 2 * BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT (double expected), e) commitment_bytes with length = 1 (minimal non-empty). 3. Set up test validator node with AddressSanitizer and crash monitoring enabled. 4. Send each malformed sidecar via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor for: a) Segfault/crash (vulnerability - DoS or memory corruption), b) AddressSanitizer buffer overflow detection, c) Clean rejection with error message (correctly handled), d) Silent acceptance with incorrect behavior (data corruption vulnerability). 6. If crash occurs, analyze stack trace to confirm it originates from commitment deserialization or KZG operations. 7. Document exact failure mode and memory safety implications.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Commitment bytes are deserialized into G1 points (48 bytes). Incorrect length could cause out-of-bounds read during deserialization.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer send cell with incorrect length to cause crash or memory corruption?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the exact value of BYTES_PER_CELL constant in the codebase (typically FIELD_ELEMENTS_PER_CELL * BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT). 2. Construct data column sidecars with malformed cell data: a) cell with length = BYTES_PER_CELL - 1 (one byte short), b) cell with length = BYTES_PER_CELL + 1 (one byte extra), c) cell with length = 0 (empty), d) cell with length = BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT (single field element instead of full cell), e) cell with length = 2 * BYTES_PER_CELL (double expected). 3. Set up test validator node with AddressSanitizer and memory debugging enabled. 4. Send each malformed sidecar via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor for: a) Crash during cell parsing or field element extraction, b) Out-of-bounds read when iterating over field elements in cell, c) Incorrect polynomial evaluation due to truncated/extended cell data, d) Proper rejection with clear error message. 6. Pay special attention to loops that iterate over cell assuming BYTES_PER_CELL / BYTES_PER_FIELD_ELEMENT elements. 7. Verify field element boundaries are not violated by malformed cell length.
checklist_item.bug_class: Buffer Overflow via Malformed Cell Length
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Cells contain multiple field elements. Incorrect cell length could cause out-of-bounds access during field element iteration.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can a malicious P2P peer send proof_bytes with incorrect length to cause crash or memory corruption?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the exact value of BYTES_PER_PROOF constant in the codebase. 2. Construct data column sidecars with malformed proof_bytes: a) proof_bytes with length = BYTES_PER_PROOF - 1 (one byte short), b) proof_bytes with length = BYTES_PER_PROOF + 1 (one byte extra), c) proof_bytes with length = 0 (empty), d) proof_bytes with length = 2 * BYTES_PER_PROOF (double expected), e) proof_bytes with length = 1 (minimal non-empty). 3. Set up test validator node with AddressSanitizer and crash monitoring enabled. 4. Send each malformed sidecar via P2P gossip protocol. 5. Monitor for: a) Segfault/crash during proof deserialization (DoS vulnerability), b) AddressSanitizer detection of buffer overflow, c) Incorrect proof parsing leading to invalid signature verification, d) Clean rejection with appropriate error (correctly handled). 6. If crash occurs, analyze stack trace to confirm it originates from proof deserialization or pairing operations. 7. Check if undersized proofs could be accepted and cause cryptographic verification to use uninitialized/garbage bytes.
checklist_item.bug_class: Buffer Overflow via Malformed Proof Length
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Proof bytes are deserialized into G1 points for KZG verification. Incorrect length could cause out-of-bounds read or use of uninitialized memory.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch validate that commitment_bytes[], cells[], and proof_bytes[] arrays have consistent lengths?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Open verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) source code. 2. Identify the batch input parameters: commitment_bytes[], cells[], cell_indices[], proof_bytes[]. 3. Check if the function validates array length consistency at entry: a) len(commitment_bytes) == len(cells) == len(cell_indices) == len(proof_bytes). 4. If lengths are mismatched: a) What happens if commitment_bytes has more elements than cells?, b) What happens if proof_bytes has fewer elements than expected?, c) Are there any loops that could go out of bounds with mismatched lengths? 5. Construct test cases with mismatched batch sizes via P2P. 6. Verify that mismatched lengths are rejected before any processing begins. 7. Check for potential integer overflow in batch size calculations.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-001. Batch functions must validate array length consistency to prevent index-out-of-bounds during batch iteration.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the P2P gossip validation layer enforce byte length constraints before data reaches verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch?

reachability.notes: Incorrect byte lengths from P2P could cause deserialization errors, buffer overflows, or incorrect field element parsing.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the P2P message handler for DataColumnSidecar. 2. Trace the deserialization and validation path from raw P2P bytes to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 3. Check if the P2P layer validates byte lengths during deserialization: a) Does SSZ schema enforce BYTES_PER_COMMITMENT length for commitment fields?, b) Does SSZ schema enforce BYTES_PER_CELL length for cell data?, c) Does SSZ schema enforce BYTES_PER_PROOF length for proof fields? 4. If P2P layer uses fixed-size types: can a malformed message bypass type constraints? 5. If P2P layer uses variable-size types: where is length validation performed? 6. Test sending raw malformed bytes that violate expected lengths at the wire protocol level. 7. Verify defense in depth: both P2P layer AND verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch should validate lengths (single point of validation is higher risk). 8. Document which layer(s) provide the actual security boundary.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-11, Trust Boundary: tb-002. Defense in depth: ideally both P2P deserialization layer and cryptographic function should validate byte lengths independently.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Does bytes_to_kzg_commitment perform BLS12-381 G1 subgroup check during deserialization?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the bytes_to_kzg_commitment deserialization function in the KZG library. 2. Locate where raw bytes are converted to BLS12-381 G1 points. 3. Verify that subgroup check is performed: a) The function should call a subgroup membership test (e.g., multiply by group order and check if result is identity), b) OR the function should use a validated deserialization that includes subgroup check. 4. Generate a test point that is ON the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup (small subgroup attack point). 5. Attempt to deserialize this non-subgroup point via bytes_to_kzg_commitment. 6. Verify the function rejects the point with a clear error (not silently accepts). 7. If accepted, construct a PoC where a forged commitment passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 8. Check if the subgroup check uses constant-time implementation to prevent timing attacks.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002, STRIDE Threat: stride-024. Missing subgroup checks in pairing-based cryptography allow forging proofs. This is a well-known attack vector (CVE-2019-11510 class).

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Does bytes_to_kzg_proof perform BLS12-381 G1 subgroup check during deserialization?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the bytes_to_kzg_proof deserialization function in the KZG library. 2. Locate where raw proof bytes are converted to BLS12-381 G1 points. 3. Verify that subgroup check is performed during proof deserialization. 4. Generate a test proof point that is ON the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. 5. Construct a DataColumnSidecar with the non-subgroup proof point. 6. Send via P2P gossip and monitor verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch behavior. 7. Verify the proof is rejected during deserialization (not after pairing). 8. If accepted, the pairing check may produce incorrect results - construct PoC demonstrating invalid proof acceptance.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002, STRIDE Threat: stride-024. Proof points also require subgroup validation. A non-subgroup proof could cause pairing to produce incorrect results.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: What is the impact on the pairing check if non-subgroup points are accepted?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Analyze the pairing equation used in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch: e(commitment - y*G1, G2) == e(proof, tau*G2 - x*G2). 2. Consider what happens when commitment or proof is a non-subgroup element: a) Non-subgroup elements have different order than expected, b) Pairing properties may not hold as expected, c) The equation could become satisfiable with forged inputs. 3. Research known small-subgroup attacks on BLS12-381 pairing. 4. Construct a specific attack scenario: a) Valid blob data, b) Invalid commitment (non-subgroup point), c) Crafted proof that makes pairing equation pass. 5. If feasible, demonstrate forged data availability proof. 6. Assess impact: Can an attacker claim data is available when it's not? Can invalid blobs pass verification?
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002. Pairing-based verification assumes all points are in the correct subgroup. Violating this assumption breaks security guarantees.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Can malicious P2P peer bypass subgroup checks via crafted DataColumnSidecar?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Map the complete path from P2P message receipt to verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 2. Identify ALL points where commitment/proof bytes are deserialized. 3. For each deserialization point: a) Is subgroup check performed?, b) Could bytes bypass this deserialization?, c) Could cached/pre-validated points be substituted? 4. Construct a DataColumnSidecar with: a) Valid SSZ encoding, b) Valid curve point (on BLS12-381), c) Non-subgroup point for commitment OR proof. 5. Send via P2P gossip protocol. 6. Monitor if the sidecar is: a) Rejected at P2P gossip validation layer (defense in depth), b) Rejected at KZG deserialization layer (primary defense), c) Accepted but fails at pairing (late detection, may cause resource waste), d) Accepted and passes pairing (VULNERABILITY). 7. Verify rejection happens early in the pipeline to minimize DoS impact.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Covered node: actor-p2p-peer. Defense in depth requires subgroup validation at multiple layers.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch assume inputs have passed subgroup validation?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Read the verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch (SG-021) implementation. 2. Identify all G1 point inputs: commitment_bytes[], proof_bytes[]. 3. Check if the function: a) Performs subgroup validation internally (good), b) Documents that callers must pre-validate (acceptable with proper enforcement), c) Silently assumes valid subgroup (DANGEROUS). 4. If callers are expected to validate, trace all call sites and verify each performs validation. 5. Check batch processing: a) Are points validated individually before batching?, b) Could a single invalid point in a batch affect others? 6. Verify the trusted setup G2 points are validated at load time (tau*G2, G2). 7. Test with mixed batch: some valid, some non-subgroup points.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Primary Element: SG-021:verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. Batch functions may have different validation characteristics than single-point functions.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Which cryptographic library implements the BLS12-381 subgroup check, and is it correct?

reachability.notes: An attacker provides commitment or proof bytes that deserialize to a point on the BLS12-381 curve but NOT in the correct G1 subgroup. Without subgroup checks, the pairing equation could give incorrect results.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the cryptographic library used for BLS12-381 operations (e.g., blst, arkworks, noble-curves). 2. Locate the subgroup check implementation in that library. 3. Verify the subgroup check method is mathematically correct: a) For G1: multiply point by r (group order) and check result is identity O, b) OR use endomorphism-based fast subgroup check. 4. Check for known CVEs or security advisories in the library version used. 5. Verify the library's subgroup check is actually called during deserialization. 6. Check if there are any code paths that bypass the library's validation (e.g., unsafe deserialization methods). 7. Test with known non-subgroup test vectors from the library's test suite.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-12, Trust Boundary: tb-002. The security of subgroup checks depends on correct library implementation and proper usage.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-002: Does commitment deduplication in verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch preserve semantic equivalence?

reachability.notes: An off-by-one in the commitment_indices mapping would cause weights to be accumulated against the wrong commitment in the verification equation.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch function (SG-021) and identify the commitment deduplication logic. 2. Trace how commitment_indices mapping is constructed from original commitments to deduplicated commitments. 3. Verify the invariant: for all k, deduplicated_commitments[commitment_indices[k]] == original_commitment_bytes[k]. 4. Check for off-by-one errors in index calculations during deduplication. 5. Construct test cases with duplicate commitments at various positions and verify correct index mapping. 6. Test edge cases: all unique commitments, all identical commitments, alternating duplicates. 7. Verify that weight accumulation in the verification equation uses correct indices. 8. Attempt to craft a malicious batch where incorrect mapping would cause acceptance of invalid proofs.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W5B14-13, Trust Boundary: tb-002, Source Invariant: SG-021:INV-003. Off-by-one in commitment_indices could cause weights to aggregate against wrong commitment, potentially accepting invalid proofs.
